docs.title: Shoal Quick Start Guide
docs.text:
Welcome to Shoal - the simplest way to deploy your application to the internet. Point Shoal at your GitHub repo, connect a proxy, and hit deploy. Shoal handles containers, scaling, SSL, and routing automatically, so you can focus on building.

This guide walks you through everything you need to get started - from core concepts to deploying apps with databases, schedulers, and multi-service routing.

docs.title: What is a proxy server? Why do I need one?
docs.text:
A proxy server sits in front of your app and routes incoming traffic to the right place. Think of it as a receptionist - visitors don't go straight to your desk, they check in first. On Shoal, your proxy handles this automatically so your app is reachable from the internet without any extra setup.

It also keeps your app secure and stable. The proxy shields your app from direct exposure to the internet, handles SSL so all traffic is encrypted, and protects against sudden spikes by rate limiting incoming requests - so a surge in traffic won't take your app down.

docs.title: What is a container?
docs.text: A container is a lightweight package that holds your app and everything it needs to run - code, dependencies, settings. It works the same way on any machine, so there are no "works on my computer" surprises. Shoal runs your app inside containers behind the scenes.

docs.title: What is a Dockerfile?
docs.text:
A Dockerfile is a simple text file that describes how to build your container. It lists the steps to set up your app - what base image to start from, what files to copy in, and what command to run. Think of it as a recipe: Shoal follows it to package your app into a container that's ready to deploy.

Coming soon: Shoal will be able to build and deploy your app without needing a Dockerfile at all.

docs.title: What is a scheduler?
docs.text: A scheduler runs tasks on a timer - like a cron job or a recurring reminder. If you need something to happen every night at midnight or every 5 minutes, a scheduler handles that for you without leaving a server running 24/7.

docs.title: Where does Shoal deploy?
docs.text: Shoal currently deploys into Google Cloud Platform (GCP). Support for Azure, AWS, and other cloud providers is coming soon - and if you want full control, you'll be able to deploy into your own private infrastructure too.

docs.title: What's a project, and an environment?
docs.text: A project is a collection of services that belong together - your app, its database, maybe a background worker. An environment is a version of that project for a specific purpose: typically development (for testing changes) and production (the live version your users see). Changes in development don't affect production until you're ready.

docs.title: Deploying an Application with a Database
docs.text:
In this example, we have an application connected to a database hosted on Neon. (Shoal will soon support its own managed databases.)

You need two components: a container node and a proxy node.

  • Container node - links to your source code, runs and scales your container, and lets you add environment variables (like your database connection string).
  • Proxy node - where you set the DNS name for your app.

Hit deploy, and it just works.

docs.title: Step One
docs.text: Drag a container node and a proxy node onto the canvas, then link them together. You can also add a comment box if you like.

docs.title: Step Two
docs.text: Click the proxy node, open the Config tab, and enter the URL name you want. For example, entering shopping-test will make your app available at shopping-test.eu1.shoal.live. You can also point a custom domain at this address.

docs.title: Step Three
docs.text:
Click the container node, open the Config tab, and set up your source - either a GitHub repo or a file upload. You'll need a Dockerfile in your code.

Add any environment variables your app needs here. For example:

DATABASE_CONNECTION = postgresql://neondb_owner:password@server.aws.neon.tech/neondb?sslmode=require

Environment variables are passed in at runtime only - they're stored securely and encrypted. You can also review, add, or remove them any time from the Settings menu in your project.

docs.title: Step Four
docs.text: Press Deploy. You can watch the deployment in real time via the Observability menu, or by clicking the link on the deploy button.

docs.title: Done
docs.text: Your app is live at the address you configured - connected to your database and running in a scalable, resilient, and protected environment.

docs.title: Deploying an Application with Multiple Containers and Routing
docs.text:
In this example, we have an application made up of several services, all exposed under one domain.

You need four components: three container nodes and one proxy node.

  • Container nodes - each links to its own source code and runs independently, with automatic scaling and resilience.
  • Proxy node - routes incoming requests to the right container based on the path or subdomain you configure.

Hit deploy, and it just works.

docs.title: Step One
docs.text: Drag a container node and a proxy node onto the canvas, then link them together. You can also add a comment box if you like.

docs.title: Step Two
docs.text: Click the proxy node, open the Config tab, and enter the URL name you want. For example, entering shopping-test will make your app available at shopping-test.eu1.shoal.live. You can also point a custom domain at this address.

docs.title: Step Three
docs.text: Click each container node, open the Config tab, and set up its source - either a GitHub repo or a file upload. You'll need a Dockerfile in each service's code.

docs.title: Step Four
docs.text:
Configure routing on the proxy node. Double-click a connection to enter the path prefix you want to route to each service. For example, if you have a finance service and a payment service:

  • /finance/ → finance container
  • /payments/ → payments container

A standard pattern for services and microservices is to include a version in the path - e.g. /finance/v1/... and /payments/v1/.... This keeps routing clean and gives your frontend a stable contract as services evolve.

docs.title: Step Five
docs.text: Press Deploy. You can watch the deployment in real time via the Observability menu, or by clicking the link on the deploy button.

docs.title: Done
docs.text: Your app is live at the address you configured - with traffic routed to the right service, running in a scalable, resilient, and protected environment.

docs.title: Deploying an Application with a Scheduler
docs.text:
In this example, we have an application that runs on a schedule - for instance, posting a daily update to a Slack channel.

You need two components: a container node and a scheduler node.

  • Container node - links to your source code and runs your container on demand.
  • Scheduler node - where you set the times Shoal should trigger your container.

Hit deploy, and it just works.

docs.title: Deploying an Application
docs.text:
Deploy in a few simple steps. In this example, we have an application we want to expose on the internet, with source code hosted on GitHub.

You need two components: a container node and a proxy node.

  • Container node - links to your source code. Shoal builds and runs your container, scales it automatically, and keeps it resilient.
  • Proxy node - where you set the DNS name (web address) you want your app to be reachable at.

Hit deploy, and it just works.

docs.title: Step One
docs.text: Drag a container node and a proxy node onto the canvas, then link them together. You can also add a comment box if you like.

docs.title: Step Two
docs.text: Click the proxy node, open the Config tab, and enter the URL name you want. For example, entering shopping-test will make your app available at shopping-test.eu1.shoal.live. You can also point a custom domain at this address.

docs.title: Step Three
docs.text: Click the container node, open the Config tab, and set up your source - either a GitHub repo or a file upload. You'll need a Dockerfile in your code.

docs.title: Step Four
docs.text: Press Deploy. You can watch the deployment in real time via the Observability menu, or by clicking the link on the deploy button.

docs.title: Done
docs.text: Your app is live at the address you configured - running in a scalable, resilient, and protected environment.

docs.title: How do I set up a custom domain?
docs.text:
To use your own domain (e.g. app.mycompany.com) instead of the default eu1.shoal.live address:

  1. In your proxy node config, note the full Shoal address for your deployment (e.g. my-app.eu1.shoal.live).

  2. Go to your DNS provider and add a CNAME record:

    Type Name Value CNAME app my-app.eu1.shoal.live

  3. DNS changes can take a few minutes to propagate. Once live, your custom domain will route to your Shoal deployment automatically.

Shoal handles SSL for custom domains - no certificate setup needed.

docs.title: Why is my deployment failing?
docs.text:
Check the Observability menu first - it shows real-time build and runtime logs and is the quickest way to find the error.

Symptom Likely cause Build fails immediately Missing or invalid Dockerfile - see Writing a Dockerfile Build succeeds but app won't start App crashing on startup - check runtime logs in Observability App starts but isn't reachable App not listening on port 8080 Environment errors at runtime Missing environment variable - check container node Config tab

If you're still stuck, the Observability log output will usually point directly at the problem.

docs.title: What are environment variables?
docs.text:
Environment variables are key-value pairs passed into your container at runtime - things like connection strings, API keys, and config values. They're stored securely and encrypted, and are never baked into your container image.

You can add, edit, or remove them from the container node Config tab or from the Settings menu in your project.

Separate secrets management with masking and access controls is coming soon.

docs.title: What port should my app listen on?
docs.text:
Shoal's gateway maps your app to the outside world via port 8080 - so your app needs to listen on that port for traffic to reach it. If your framework defaults to something different, use the reference below to change it.

Coming soon: Shoal will handle this mapping automatically so you won't need to configure it yourself.

docs.title: Node.js
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Express 3000 app.listen(8080) Fastify 3000 fastify.listen({ port: 8080, host: '0.0.0.0' }) NestJS 3000 app.listen(8080) Koa none app.listen(8080) Hapi none port: 8080 in server config

docs.title: Python
docs.text: Framework Router / Server Default Port How to use port 8080 Flask Werkzeug 5000 app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=8080) Django Gunicorn / runserver 8000 CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8080"] FastAPI Uvicorn 8000 CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8080"] Tornado Built-in none app.listen(8080)

docs.title: Go
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Gin 8080 Already default - r.Run(":8080") Echo 1323 e.Start(":8080") Fiber 3000 app.Listen(":8080") Chi none http.ListenAndServe(":8080", r) net/http (stdlib) none http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)

docs.title: Java
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Spring Boot 8080 Already default Quarkus 8080 Already default Micronaut 8080 Already default Vert.x 8888 vertx.createHttpServer().listen(8080)

docs.title: C#
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 ASP.NET Core 5000 Set env var ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://+:8080 in container node config Minimal API 5000 Set env var ASPNETCORE_URLS=http://+:8080 in container node config

docs.title: PHP
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Laravel 8000 CMD ["php", "artisan", "serve", "--host=0.0.0.0", "--port=8080"] Symfony 8000 CMD ["php", "-S", "0.0.0.0:8080", "-t", "public"] Slim none $app->run() uses server config - set via CMD ["php", "-S", "0.0.0.0:8080"]

docs.title: Ruby
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Rails (Puma) 3000 CMD ["rails", "server", "-b", "0.0.0.0", "-p", "8080"] Sinatra 4567 set :port, 8080 and set :bind, '0.0.0.0'

docs.title: Elixir
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Phoenix 4000 Set env var PORT=8080 - Phoenix reads it by default

docs.title: Kotlin
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Ktor none embeddedServer(Netty, port = 8080, host = "0.0.0.0") Spring Boot (Kotlin) 8080 Already default

docs.title: Rust
docs.text: Framework Default Port How to use port 8080 Actix-web none .bind("0.0.0.0:8080")? Axum none TcpListener::bind("0.0.0.0:8080").await? Rocket 8000 Add port = 8080 under [default] in Rocket.toml Warp none warp::serve(routes).run(([0, 0, 0, 0], 8080)).await

docs.title: Can I deploy a private GitHub repo?
docs.text: Yes. When connecting a GitHub repo in the container node config, Shoal will prompt you to authorise access to your GitHub account. You can grant access to specific repositories - including private ones - without giving Shoal access to your entire account.

docs.title: What is eu1.shoal.live?
docs.text:
When you deploy on Shoal, your app is given a subdomain on eu1.shoal.live - for example, my-app.eu1.shoal.live. This is Shoal's shared domain for the EU1 region. It's live immediately after deploy, no DNS setup required.

You can use this address directly, or point your own custom domain at it - see How do I set up a custom domain?.

docs.title: FAQ & Guides
docs.text: Common questions and guides for getting started with Shoal.

docs.title: Guides
docs.text:
• How to zip your project folder
  • Writing a Dockerfile

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text:
• What is eu1.shoal.live?
  • How do I set up a custom domain?
  • What are environment variables?
  • What port should my app listen on?
  • Why is my deployment failing?
  • Can I deploy a private GitHub repo?

docs.title: Initial Setup
docs.text:
After logging in and selecting your project, you can create a new environment (if one does not exist) using the Create button.

Once your environment is ready, head to one of the deployment guides to get your app live.

Just select the environment from the list to login to see the canvas.

docs.title: Writing a Dockerfile
docs.text:
Shoal uses Docker to package and run your app in a container. A Dockerfile is a plain text file that describes how to do that - it tells Shoal what language runtime to use, how to install your dependencies, and how to start your app.

Without a Dockerfile, Shoal can't build your container. (Support for automatic detection without a Dockerfile is coming soon.)

docs.title: Where does it go?
docs.text:
The Dockerfile must be in the root of your project - the same top-level folder you're uploading or pointing your GitHub repo at.

my-project/
├── Dockerfile        ← here
├── main.py           (or index.js, main.go, etc.)
├── requirements.txt  (or package.json, go.mod, etc.)
└── ...

Shoal looks for it there automatically when you deploy. You don't need to configure the path.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Here are simple starting-point Dockerfiles for common languages.

PythonNode.jsGo 
FROM python:3.12-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

COPY . .

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["python", "main.py"]

Make sure your app listens on port 8080, or update EXPOSE and your start command to match.

FROM node:20-slim

WORKDIR /app

COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev

COPY . .

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["node", "index.js"]

Replace index.js with your app's entry point if it differs.

FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN go build -o server .

FROM alpine:latest

WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /app/server .

EXPOSE 8080

CMD ["./server"]

This uses a two-stage build - the final image contains only your compiled binary, keeping it small.

docs.title: How to Zip Your Project Folder
docs.text:
Shoal accepts a zip file of your project when you don't want to connect a GitHub repo directly. Here's how to create one on each platform.

MacLinuxWindows

Using Finder:

  1. Locate your project folder in Finder.
  2. Right-click the folder.
  3. Select Compress "folder-name".

A .zip file will appear in the same location.

Using Terminal:

zip -r myapp.zip myapp/

Replace myapp with your folder name.

Using the terminal:

zip -r myapp.zip myapp/

Replace myapp with your folder name. If zip is not installed:

# Debian / Ubuntu
sudo apt install zip

# Fedora / RHEL
sudo dnf install zip

Using File Explorer:

  1. Locate your project folder.
  2. Right-click the folder.
  3. Select Send to → Compressed (zipped) folder.

Using PowerShell:

Compress-Archive -Path .\myapp -DestinationPath .\myapp.zip

Replace myapp with your folder name.
